sentences.base_language: Today I want to cook at home.
sentences.romanized: Jīntiān wǒ xiǎng zài jiā zuòfàn.
sentences.grammarNote: Time words like 今天 (jīntiān, today) come at the very beginning of the sentence in Chinese — before the subject or right after it. Never at the end as in English.

sentences.base_language: First, I go to buy vegetables.
sentences.grammarNote: 去 + verb is a common pattern meaning 'go (somewhere) to do something'. 去买 = 'go buy'. The destination is implied or stated separately.

sentences.base_language: I need two tomatoes and some garlic.
sentences.target_language: 我需要两个西红柿和一些大蒜。
sentences.romanized: Wǒ xūyào liǎng gè xīhóngshì hé yīxiē dàsuàn.
sentences.grammarNote: 个 (gè) is the most common general-purpose measure word for objects. 两 (liǎng) is used instead of 二 (èr) when counting things with a measure word: 两个, 两本, 两杯.

sentences.base_language: I put the oil in the pan.
sentences.romanized: Wǒ bǎ yóu fàng jìn guō lǐ.
sentences.grammarNote: 把 (bǎ) construction: 把 + object + verb + result/direction. It emphasizes what happens TO the object. '把油放进锅里' = 'Take the oil and put it into the pan.' 把 sentences require a verb with a result or direction (进, 好, 完, etc.).
verb + 起来 describes how something seems when you do the action: 看起来 (looks like), 听起来 (sounds like), 闻起来 (smells like). It's a resultative complement meaning 'upon doing it'.

完 (wán) as a resultative complement after a verb means the action is finished/completed: 做完 = 'finish making', 吃完 = 'finish eating', 看完 = 'finish reading'. The 了 at the end confirms the completed state.
